questions.prompt: A customer asks for a $30 refund in good faith. Your policy refuses; they dispute. You win the dispute with strong evidence but spent 4 weeks + $25 fee. What did you learn?

choices.text: Refund first; the math almost always favors it. A $30 refund vs. $30 + $25 dispute fee + admin time + reputation hit on a 'won' dispute means the dispute cost you more than the refund would have, even when you win. Policy: in-good-faith refund requests get refunded promptly.

questions.explanation: Dispute economics favor refunds. The fee + time + reputation impact of even a won dispute usually exceeds the refund amount. Set support policy: refund in-good-faith requests; reserve disputes for clear fraud.

questions.prompt: What's the highest-leverage thing to do FIRST in dispute prevention?

choices.text: Fix your billing descriptor to clearly match your brand name (and ideally include support URL). 'PAY*OURCORP-22Q' looks like a scam; 'Acme Inc · support.acme.com' is recognizable. A confused customer sees an unknown charge and disputes; a clear descriptor prevents that confusion entirely. Combine with email receipts that arrive immediately after purchase.

questions.explanation: Billing descriptors are often the highest-ROI dispute prevention. A cryptic descriptor causes 'fraudulent' disputes from confused customers who don't recognize the charge. Clear descriptor + receipt email = problem largely solved.
